text: Profiling cell identity and tissue architecture with single-cell and spatial transcriptomics. Gunsagar S. Gulati, Jeremy Philip D'Silva, Yunhe Liu, Linghua Wang & Aaron M. Newman. Abstract. Single-cell transcriptomics has broadened our understanding of cellular diversity and gene expression dynamics in healthy and diseased tissues. Recently, spatial transcriptomics has emerged as a tool to contextualize single cells in multicellular neighbourhoods and to identify spatially recurrent phenotypes, or ecotypes. These technologies have generated vast datasets with targeted-transcriptome and whole-transcriptome profiles of hundreds to millions of cells. Such data have provided new insights into developmental hierarchies, cellular plasticity and diverse tissue microenvironments, and spurred a burst of innovation in computational methods for single-cell analysis. In this Review, we discuss recent advancements, ongoing challenges and prospects in identifying and characterizing cell states and multicellular neighbourhoods. We discuss recent progress in sample processing, data integration, identification of subtle cell states, trajectory modelling, deconvolution and spatial analysis. Furthermore, we discuss the increasing application of deep learning, including foundation models, in analysing single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data. Finally, we discuss recent applications of these tools in the fields of stem cell biology, immunology, and tumour biology, and the future of single-cell and spatial transcriptomics in biological research and its translation to the clinic.

Introduction. Since its inception nearly 15 years ago, single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has transformed our fundamental understanding of cellular heterogeneity and gene expression dynamics across various species, tissues and stages of life. This methodology has enabled the construction of large cell atlases, discovery of rare cell types and states, and mapping of cell trajectories during complex biological processes such as early embryogenesis, brain development and cancer growth. RNA can be profiled from individual cells at an unprecedented scale and resolution, integrated with genomic and proteomic assays, and measured in situ to reveal the intricate spatial organization and functional relationships between neighbouring cells within tissues. The Human Cell Atlas, which is part of the broader effort to collect observational scRNA-seq data across tissues and species, is often compared to the pioneering Human Genome Project, an analogy that underscores its potential to spur technological development, uncover biological diversity and improve disease treatments. Although high-dimensional single-cell technologies present extraordinary opportunities for discovery, they also involve challenges in experimental design and bioinformatics. In this Review, we discuss the latest advances and challenges in single-cell transcriptomics as they pertain to sample preparation, data integration and correction, the curation of large cell atlases, identification of rare and subtle cell states, inferring cellular trajectories, and predicting developmental potential. We then discuss technical and biological considerations in spatial transcriptomics (ST) and methods to integrate scRNA-seq with ST. We introduce the concept of spatial ecotypes as groups of spatially colocalized and phenotypically coherent cell states and discuss its relevance for characterizing normal and diseased tissue microenvironments. As scRNA-seq and ST are generating large volumes of high-dimensional data, deconvolution methods and simpler assays, such as immunohistochemistry and gene expression signatures, can facilitate their translation to large clinical cohorts. Moreover, with the exponential rise in scale and complexity of single-cell genomic data, we comment on the potential use of foundation models in single-cell biology and offer our perspective on how existing and emerging deep learning technologies will transform the field of single-cell and spatial transcriptomics.

section: Single-cell transcriptomics: sample processing and batch correction
text: Single-cell transcriptomics. Although scRNA-seq has revolutionized our understanding of the biology of multicellular organisms, inattention to the effects of sample preparation, data normalization and batch integration can lead to false conclusions and misinterpretation of results. In this section, we discuss key technical strategies and in silico tools to extract meaningful insights from scRNA-seq data while minimizing artifacts. With precise data acquisition and processing, scRNA-seq becomes a powerful tool to identify novel cell types and states, elucidate intercellular relationships and uncover intrinsic cellular properties with clinical potential. Identifying technical and biological covariates in sample processing and dissociation. Sample preparation, cell dissociation and RNA capture are the first, and perhaps least standardized, steps in generating scRNA-seq data. Transcriptional and other cellular changes occur as soon as an organism dies. Within minutes to hours, tissue composition changes as immune-cell extravasation mechanisms deteriorate and vulnerable cells undergo apoptosis. RNA synthesis shuts down, reducing the amount of detectable transcripts per cell, and nonrandom transcriptional alterations occur, including tissue-specific variation in mitochondrial RNA content, splicing dysregulation and increased production of alternative isoforms, and gene inactivation related to carbohydrate metabolism, immune responses, cell cycling, stress responses and cell necrosis. Similar time-dependent changes are observed when using different dissociation protocols, varying by the types of digestion enzymes and incubation temperatures for dissociation. Several tissue-intrinsic and cell-intrinsic features can also affect cellular composition during dissociation. Chemicals in tissues, such as stomach acid, bile acids and intestinal and pancreatic enzymes, can directly lyse cells and degrade RNA during isolation. Adherent cells that are difficult to detach, such as fibroblasts, large cells with filopodia, such as macrophages and adipocytes, and short-lived cells, such as neutrophils, can be easily missed or filtered out during dissociation. Enzymatic digestion also degrades cell surface antigens, thereby reducing antibody-mediated detectability during flow cytometry and other related methods, such as CITE-seq and REAP-seq. These cellular and transcriptional changes can have profound effects on downstream analyses such as low-dimensional embedding, clustering, differential gene expression, and lineage tracing. Experimental strategies have been developed to mitigate these dissociation biases, but they have introduced new confounders in exchange. Single-nucleus RNA-seq (snRNA-seq) isolates nuclear RNA from frozen and difficult-to-dissociate tissues, such as neurons and glial cells. However, isolating nuclei from different organisms, tissues and cells of interest still requires customized protocols to minimize dissociation biases. Nuclei isolation for transcriptional profiling is susceptible to contamination with ambient RNA and excludes key cytoplasmic transcripts, including those involved in microglial activation regulatory RNA stored in processing bodies, and nuclear RNA during mitosis (when cells lack a nuclear membrane). Chemical fixation can immediately preserve the cellular and transcriptional contents of the tissue of interest, but it also corrupts the ultrastructure of cells and their contents, making it challenging to perform whole-transcriptome sequencing. The 10x Genomics Flex Kit extracts RNA from fixed tissues using predetermined probe sets in mouse and human. Although this protocol minimizes the effect of sample processing and technical batch on data quality, RNA quantification is limited to curated genomic regions and dissociation of single-cell nuclei remains susceptible to distortions in cell composition. ST, which is discussed in detail below, can preserve the tissue context of cells without dissociation, potentially providing results that are more physiologically representative. Reducing sample preparation time, optimizing digestion buffers and developing community standards for these pre-processing steps will be crucial to minimizing experimental variability. Post-hoc correction of technical covariates using computational tools, as described below, can improve the biological relevance of scRNA-seq data, but it does not obviate the need for diligent annotation of pre-processing variables and the design of control groups to accurately eliminate confounding factors without diminishing the biological signal. Computational strategies for sample integration and batch correction. In single-cell experiments involving multiple samples, biological differences of interest between samples can be obscured by unintended technical and biological covariates that affect downstream analysis. Variation in sample preparation and processing, as described above, and sequencing-related factors, such as library size, PCR cycles and sequencing instrument, can introduce artifacts in the data, resulting in single cells clustering more by technical batch than by biological similarity. Other factors related to the subject, such as the age, sex and ancestry of the sampled individuals or heterogeneity in features, such as cell cycle stage or mitochondrial RNA content, can overshadow the biological variables of interest. Many computational methods have been developed to correct batch-specific variation while aiming to preserve true biological signal. ComBat and limma are popular linear adjustment models used for microarray and bulk RNA-seq data, but their application to scRNA-seq data has been challenging owing to data sparsity and to variability in cell composition between batches. This limitation motivated the development of methods such as mutual nearest neighbours (MNN), which identifies neighbourhoods of similar cells across batches and uses these alignments to integrate batches and correct gene expression data. To expedite the search for shared elements across batches and improve robustness to noise, gene expression profiles can be reduced to low-dimensional embeddings using methods such as principal components analysis (for example, fastMNN, BBKNN, Harmony), singular value decomposition (for example, Scanorama), integrative non-negative matrix factorization (for example, LIGER), or canonical correlation analysis (for example, Seurat) to more efficiently identify and align similar cells. These methods can require significant user input and parameter tuning, so prior knowledge and a trial-and-error strategy not overly reliant on default settings are encouraged for optimal results. Although data integration tools improve visualization, clustering and downstream analysis, they risk overcorrecting and eliminating desired biological signals. One strategy to preserve biological signal is to use semi-supervised methods, which can be informed by cell annotations. Semi-supervised deep learning methods, including scANVI and scGEN, were shown to outperform unsupervised methods in batch correction and preservation of biological variation. Computational tools alone are probably insufficient to completely distinguish biological signals from confounding variables. Standardization in the design of scRNA-seq experiments and the proactive inclusion of technical control groups, from which batch effects can be learned and applied to cases (for example, pool-of-controls) will be needed to accurately model cross-sample variation.

section: Cell atlases, rare cell states, and trajectory inference
text: Construction and curation of large single-cell atlases. In recent years, we have witnessed an exponential rise in the number and diversity of transcriptionally profiled single cells, owing to several large cell atlas efforts spanning many tissues and organisms. Remarkably, about 80% of these 128 million or more single-cell transcriptomes were sequenced in the past 3 years, covering at least 190 tissues and 76 species. The ultimate goal of these atlas-creation projects is to construct an annotated, publicly accessible dictionary of cell types and states that can serve as a reference for cell labelling, transcriptional signatures, disease association and hypothesis generation. Despite recent progress, challenges in quality control and data integration across variable sample processing and sequencing modalities persist. To address these problems, massive efforts are underway to protocolize single-cell experimental and computational workflows, especially for tumour samples through the Human Tumor Atlas Network. Several strategies exist for annotating single-cell data using reference data, including marker gene detection, gene set enrichment analysis, correlation-based techniques, general-purpose classifiers and maximum-likelihood estimators, among others. Large language models, such as GPT4, can effortlessly annotate cells based on a chain of prompts such as user-provided marker genes. Transfer learning strategies using deep learning models are also being developed to map annotations from single-cell references to small-scale scRNA-seq datasets and discover disease-specific cell states. Challenges in single-cell reference curation include the need for more detailed cell-level and sample-level annotations, the establishment of a unified cell state ontology encompassing various tissues, organs, and disease conditions, and the precise alignment of query datasets to condition-matched reference atlases. Experimental and computational strategies for identifying rare and subtle cell states using scRNA-seq. A major goal of scRNA-seq is the identification of rare and subtle cell populations not otherwise detectable in bulk samples. Although scRNA-seq has led to the discovery of tissue-resident stem cells and progenitor cells, immune drivers of disease and tumour cell lineage states, current experimental and computational methodologies still face many challenges in identifying rare and subtle cell states. Harsh dissociation conditions may eliminate vulnerable cells or induce stress-related changes in cell state, leading to the loss of rare and subtle cell populations during pre-processing steps. For example, standard dissociation protocols induce a unique stress response and activate quiescent muscle stem cells, resulting in poor characterization of the quiescent stem cell population and misclassification of this dissociation artifact as a new cell state. Another major factor is their frequency within the overall study population and whether the number of sequenced cells is sufficient to detect them. Tissue-resident stem cells, for instance, are exceptionally rare, and even large-scale scRNA-seq of unpurified tissues can miss these cells without a targeted enrichment strategy. For example, expression of the gene Hoxb5 marks a subpopulation of mouse long-term haematopoietic stem cell (LT-HSCs) with high regeneration capacity. Hoxb5-expressing LT-HSCs reside in the bone marrow at an estimated frequency of 0.001%, so approximately one million nucleated cells from the mouse bone marrow would be required to isolate 10 Hoxb5 LT-HSCs without prior enrichment. By enriching for specific cell-surface markers, such as KIT and SCA-1, while depleting cells expressing mature cell-surface markers, the frequency of Hoxb5 LT-HSCs is increased 1,000-fold, significantly enhancing their detectability. Other scRNA-seq studies of stem cells have similarly leveraged known biological features of cell distribution, such as surface markers, anatomic location and embryonic stage, to isolate and functionally validate human neural and skeletal stem cells. Identifying rare and subtle cell states often depends on detecting modest but reproducible differences. Plate-based full-length sequencing, although costlier and more time-consuming than droplet-based short-read sequencing, offers deeper and broader RNA capture from individual cells. Moreover, the lower dropout rates and accurate read estimation provided by full-length sequences preserve the small but crucial transcriptional differences between cells. After candidate cells and their transcripts are captured, single-cell transcriptomes are parsed to find key features indicative of biological heterogeneity. Proper feature selection is critical for the identification of rare or subtle cell states, as omission of key marker genes during this step can compromise cell state discovery. Many analysis pipelines use dispersion, or the variability in gene expression levels across individual cells in a dataset, to prioritize genes. However, these measures can be skewed by irrelevant covariates and can be insensitive to biological signals from rare or subtly different cell states. After filtering genes for high dispersion, various computational tools apply hierarchical clustering (for example, RaceID3), density measurement (for example, FiRE), or inter-cluster distance (for example, GapClust) to find rare or subtle cell states. Other feature selection strategies include GiniClust3 (Gini coefficient), CellSius (bimodal distribution), and scPNMF (projective non-negative matrix factorization). Deep generative models, such as scLDS2, are also being developed, which can flexibly learn the distribution of single cells and utilize an interpretable classifier to infer rare cells. Tracing single cells through biological transitions. scRNA-seq analysis has enabled the precise delineation of the transcriptional paths that cells undergo during dynamic biological processes, such as development, differentiation, the cell cycle, and responses to perturbations. Predicting the relative order of studied single cells in a continuum of transitional cell states using transcriptional data is a major biological challenge. Several computational and genomic methods have been developed to predict the order of single cells across diverse biological processes. Trajectory inference tools involve the generation of a low-dimensional embedding and delineation of a path through nodes identified through graph-based or clustering-based methods. As datasets grow in both size and complexity, several challenges have emerged. Trajectory inference tools are susceptible to the effects of unwanted covariates on dimensional reduction and clustering, and they rely on accurate data integration and batch correction to draw trajectories across multiple samples. Several methods have been developed to unbiasedly orient the direction of transcriptional evolution. RNA velocity analyses estimate the rate and direction of cell transitions by comparing newly transcribed pre-mRNA to mature mRNA; protacel additionally incorporates protein velocity to give richer insight into cell state dynamics when multi-omics data are available. These tools capture cell transitions occurring over short time scales (minutes to hours) but are less reliable in capturing developmental trajectories that occur over longer time scales (days to months). Ultimately, lineage tracing with single-cell resolution barcoding offers ground-truth information on the sequence of cell transitions using engineered genomic labels such as recombination events (PolyLox), viral integration (TraceSeq), CRISPR-based barcoding or natural markers including somatic mutations, mitochondrial DNA variants and DNA methylation patterns.

section: Cellular plasticity, developmental potential, and Spatial transcriptomics introduction
text: Measuring cellular plasticity. Cellular plasticity refers to the diversity of potential fates that a cell can adopt, independently of genomic alterations. It is a key feature of normal cellular responses to disease and an adaptation of cancer cells during tumorigenesis and therapy evasion. Several computational methods have been developed to quantify cell plasticity using single-cell transcriptomics. Plasticity can be conceptualized as a probabilistic process in a low-dimensional embedding, wherein the likelihood of any given cell to reach the terminal points in the graph can be used to estimate potential. Palantir, VIA and MARGARET are methods that infer complex topographies, identify terminal points and use a Markov chain model to assign probabilities to each cell based on its likelihood to reach the defined terminal points. Cells with equal probabilities of reaching each terminal state are deemed to have greater potential than those with skewed probabilities. CellRank improves upon this with an ensemble approach that leverages unbiased tools, such as RNA velocity and CytoTRACE, to infer the origins and destinations of cell states and then applies a Markov chain model to compute fate probabilities. Cell plasticity can also be studied as a function of master regulatory genes and their downstream expression networks. CellOracle leverages knowledge of gene regulatory networks derived from single-cell multi-omics analyses to perform in silico transcription-factor perturbations and demonstrate fate alterations. Measuring developmental potential. Developmental potential is a category of plasticity that measures the ability of a cell to give rise to more differentiated cells. Cells with the ability to give rise to an entire organism, such as a zygote, have the highest developmental potential, whereas terminally differentiated cells, such as neutrophils, have the lowest. Developmental potential is a hallmark of stem cells and progenitor cells during tissue growth, homoeostasis and regeneration. One strategy to predicting developmental potential is measuring the transcriptional programmes active during early embryogenesis. Examples of these tools include ORIGINS (protein-protein network), mRNAsi (logistic regression model trained on pluripotent stem cells), and PluriNet (gene set derived from protein-protein network). FitDevo expands this scope by training a generalized linear model from datasets that include both fetal and adult cells during differentiation. Single-cell analysis has also shown that chromatin accessibility and transcriptional diversity are surrogate measures of developmental potential. Intuitively, cells with promiscuous expression across the genome tend to have a higher potential to transition to different downstream cell states than those with limited transcriptional diversity. CytoTRACE demonstrated that a simple measure - the total number of genes expressed by a single cell - recapitulates cellular ontogeny from a fertilized zygote to terminally differentiated cells across various tissues and species. This transcriptional promiscuity correlated with genome-wide chromatin accessibility as calculated by ATAC-seq. Transcriptional diversity can also be quantified by computing Shannon entropy, a popular measure of uncertainty or randomness in data.Spatial transcriptomics. Cells reside in a complex microenvironment composed of extracellular molecules, structural matrices and neighbouring cells, which collectively shape the phenotypes of cells and the functional properties of tissues. Although scRNA-seq continues to expand the dictionary of cell states across a wide range of organisms and tissues, it fails to capture the context of microenvironments that nurture specific cell identities. ST has emerged as a method to simultaneously measure the transcriptional states of a cell of interest and those of its surrounding neighbours. By expanding the scope of single-cell biology from isolated cells to multicellular neighbourhoods, ST has revealed recurrent and functional organizations of cells in different tissues and conditions. Characterizing the spatial arrangement of cell states. A wide variety of methods have been developed to generate ST data, which differ in cellular resolution, transcriptomic coverage, tissue compatibility, cost, infrastructure, and availability for commercial use. Methods using fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), such as Vizgen MERSCOPE, NanoString CosMx and seqFISH, capture hundreds to thousands of transcripts at subcellular resolution by combining serial hybridization of pre-designed probes with high-resolution imaging. Other probe-based methods, such as STARmap and 10x Genomics Xenium, use in situ sequencing as a readout. Because these methods currently require creating a customized gene panel and validating gene-specific probes, they are most useful when prior knowledge is available to select marker genes for the cell states of interest. Whole-transcriptome coverage is desirable when the cell states of interest are not yet defined or when existing gene markers fail to distinguish rare and subtle cell states. A set of methods, which we refer to as bulk ST, perform whole-transcriptome sequencing in defined sample regions of varying spatial resolution. These include laser capture microscopy followed by bulk RNA-seq (LCM-seq) and array-based ST platforms, such as 10x Genomics Visium, Slide-seq V2 and Stereo-seq, which capture RNA from spots with diameters of 55 micrometers, 10 micrometers and 220 nm respectively, as well as 10x Genomics Visium HD, with a resolution of 2 micrometers square. Whereas smaller spot diameters improve single-cell resolution, this improvement is counterbalanced by lower transcript capture efficiency.

section: Spatial transcriptomics: computational tools, multicellular neighbourhoods, and spatial ecotypes
text: Overcoming technological barriers with computational tools. Several computational methods have been developed to address the inherent limitations of ST technologies, which include cellular resolution, transcript coverage and the alignment of independent samples. For example, one common strategy involves the use of reference scRNA-seq atlases with matched biological and experimental characteristics to improve ST resolution or transcript coverage. Although standard bulk RNA-seq deconvolution tools can be applied to bulk ST, dedicated tools have been developed that leverage scRNA-seq data to dissect spatial spots, either into cell type-specific fractions (for example, cell2location, RCTD), individual single-cell transcriptomes (for example, CytoSPACE, Tangram and CellTrek), sub-spot or pixel-level expression (for example, BayesSpace, XFuse, TESLA and iStar), or inferred cell type-specific expression profiles. Recent studies have also shown that integrating histology with ST data using deep learning methods can enhance both the spatial and phenotypic resolution of ST deconvolution tools. For probe-based ST, which typically measures a subset of the transcript at single-cell resolution, several methods exist for imputing the expression of unmeasured genes. Another key computational challenge is the construction of cohesive spatial atlases from multiple 2D ST samples, either from adjacent tissues or across a time series. Alignment methods can be used to integrate 2D ST data into common coordinate frameworks when they exist, or even to approximately align 2D sections from similar anatomical regions across different organisms. Discovering recurrent spatial organization by comparing multicellular neighbourhoods. Extending the study of single cells to multicellular hubs of interacting cells is improving our understanding of a wide variety of biological mechanisms, including cell plasticity, fate determination and tumour immunology. Although histological structures offer a starting point for ST analysis, they may not enable a detailed analysis of how a cell's microenvironment determines its phenotype. A multicellular neighbourhood, consisting of a cell and its local microenvironment, is the fundamental unit for ST data analysis. A multicellular neighbourhood should be defined in a data-driven manner by considering the technology and the scale of the phenomenon under study. In the context of bulk ST data, a multicellular neighbourhood could be defined as a single spot in the array, a metaspot (a spot and its nearest neighbours), or the deconvolution results of one or more spatial spots. For ST data at single-cell resolution, many definitions of a multicellular neighbourhood use either a fixed radius (50-200 micrometers), a fixed number of neighbouring cells (10-200 nearest neighbours) in 2D or 3D space, or neighbourhoods defined by Delaunay triangulation. Selecting the appropriate set of features with which to characterize multicellular neighbourhoods is a data-dependent and application-dependent process. For bulk ST, if each spot is considered a multicellular neighbourhood, the gene expression values of a spot are the natural features on which downstream analyses like clustering are performed. For single-cell-resolution ST data, a multicellular neighbourhood can by represented by its composition, that is, the abundances of cell types and states that are selected a priori and defined using known markers. This approach has the expressive power to identify histologically distinct regions, identify compositionally distinct regions that exhibit distinct intercellular signalling patterns, capture similarities and differences in tissue architecture between different lymphoid organs, and identify multicellular neighbourhood motifs associated with treatment outcomes in retrospective cancer cohorts. Other strategies aggregate gene expression values across cells to represent the multicellular neighbourhood, utilizing methods such as weighted averaging, gene-gene covariance matrices or deep learning. Analysing multicellular neighbourhoods using gene expression values can enable automated identification of cell types or context-dependent transcriptional states not present in the reference dataset. Additionally, gene expression representations are suitable for cell-cell interaction analyses in spatial data. Representations that incorporate gene expression states can encode more complexity than cell type frequencies, enabling researchers to distinguish between cellular communities with otherwise similar cell type composition. For example, the maturation status of a tertiary lymphoid structure can be accurately determined by jointly considering the transcriptional states, cellular composition and the spatial organization of the cells within the structure. A potential drawback of expression features is the presence of batch effects, in which multicellular neighbourhoods separate primarily by technical covariates (for example, sample) rather than biological features. After creating a representation of multicellular neighbourhoods, an important goal is to identify recurrent cell state communities across spatial domains, samples, or individuals. We refer to recurrent multicellular neighbourhood phenotypes, characterized by correlated cell states, as spatial ecotypes. This concept draws from the ecological notion of ecotypes as spatially dependent subpopulations of species and from work that introduced the term ecotypes for correlated transcriptional states in the tumour microenvironment. The definitions of transcriptional ecotypes and spatial ecotypes also coincide with multicellular programmes, which are sets of transcriptional states across various cell types that are co-associated in multiple samples or spatial regions. The fundamental insight shared by these studies is that one can identify associated cell states by determining the cell states whose frequencies are correlated across many independent samples or spatial regions. Spatial ecotypes and related notions of recurrent multicellular neighbourhoods have been particularly useful in the study of cancer, wherein they can be applied to identify recurrent phenotypes in the absence of well-defined histological features.

section: Clinical translation, AI and foundation models, and Conclusion
text: Translating single-cell and spatial transcriptomics into the clinic. To date, scRNA-seq has been applied in the investigation of over 103 different human diseases and incorporated into more than 58 haematology, oncology and immunology clinical trials. The construction of large cell atlases at single-cell resolution from patient samples is poised to catalyse a shift towards precision medicine, in which the presence and proportion of certain cells and transcripts will guide disease classification, therapy and prognosis. Single-cell RNA-seq is already being used to elucidate mechanisms of disease progression and spread, identify rare or subtle cell states associated with poor outcomes, and reveal potential therapeutic vulnerabilities. For example, single-cell profiling of the tumour microenvironment has revealed clinically relevant immune and non-immune phenotypes in multiple cancer types; it has also enabled the characterization of quiescent and treatment-resistant cancer stem cells and circulating tumour cells with prognostic and targetable features. Single-cell transcriptomics has also facilitated the description of normal human neural and skeletal stem cells and progenitor cells and provided high-resolution roadmaps for differentiating stem cells to desired cell fates for eventual cell transplant applications. Although initially discovered in small cohorts, findings from scRNA-seq can be externally validated and measured at scale by deconvolving cell composition from bulk RNA admixtures in large clinical databases using digital cytometry (deconvolution). Indeed, the direct utilization of scRNA-seq in the clinic is hindered by other factors, including cost, standardization and tissue dissociation artifacts. However, scRNA-seq can prioritize transcripts, cell states and proteins for evaluation using simpler assays already available in clinical settings, such as immunohistochemistry, FISH, flow cytometry and multi-gene panel sequencing. ST serves a similar role in discovering clinically relevant spatially defined cell states and ecotypes, which can then be summarized by simpler assays to guide clinical decision making. Spatial biomarkers have been shown to correlate with clinical outcomes and treatment responses in several cancers, including brain, breast, colorectal and lung cancers. Gene expression signatures of two spatial ecotypes, associated with the tumour boundary in oral squamous cell carcinoma and epithelial-mesenchymal transition in non-small-cell lung cancer, respectively, predicted survival in large held-out bulk RNA-seq cohorts. Spatial patterns of immune interactions between PD-1+ T cells and PD-L1+ myeloid cells have provided insight into immune evasion in colorectal cancer. Technological advances in the application of scRNA-seq and ST to paraffin-embedded tissues are enabling the retrospective analysis of clinical samples stored in biobanks. Large efforts such as the Human Protein Atlas are creating platforms for biomarker discovery by integrating protein, transcriptional and geospatial features with biological and clinical annotations.Artificial intelligence and the future of single-cell transcriptomics. As scRNA-seq and ST datasets continue to grow in depth, scale, resolution and complexity, parallel leaps in artificial intelligence (AI) are heralding a new era of machine-assisted single-cell biology. The rise of deep learning in single-cell transcriptomics has been facilitated by the increase in data availability, by the development of new model architectures (for example, transformers and vision transformers), and by software that enables non-experts to train, apply and interpret deep learning models. Deep learning can be applied to diverse problems depending on the model architecture (for example, convolutional neural networks and graph neural networks) or training framework (supervised or unsupervised). For example, supervised methods can predict differences in treatment outcomes from subtle molecular or spatial patterns, and unsupervised methods can uncover shared biological features across diverse datasets without requiring manual batch correction. Foundation models have received considerable recent interest in the single-cell transcriptomics research community and beyond. Foundation models are deep learning models trained on large amounts of unlabelled data, intended to form a foundation for downstream applications, either applied without any additional training or fine-tuned using a small amount of labelled data to adapt the model for a specific task. Foundation models for single-cell transcriptomics are typically trained across many datasets. One hypothesis motivating the use of foundation models is that these models automatically learn to organize scRNA-seq data from diverse platforms, tissues and conditions into a coherent embedding. Within the foundation model embedding, researchers can search for cells from other datasets that resemble their input cell, perform data integration across datasets, transfer labels (such as cell types or states) from a labelled dataset to an unlabelled one, or predict the effects of perturbations on the transcriptional state of a cell. Another hypothesis is that pre-training a model on a large amount of unlabelled data improves its ability to learn from a small amount of labelled data. The development of ST foundation models has been complicated by the relative lack of organized, large-scale atlases. A recent preprint reports the training of single-cell foundation models on scRNA-seq and ST data. This work suggests that foundation model embeddings encode information about the microenvironmental context of single cells; similar models could be trained to explicitly incorporate the microenvironmental context contained in ST data and the transcriptional data of scRNA-seq, which could enable spatially aware foundation models that impute multicellular neighbourhoods for scRNA-seq data. As with scRNA-seq, ST foundation models could be biased by imbalanced training data; an additional challenge with ST data is the prevalence of probe-based ST, for which panels are biased towards specific genes. We also foresee the development of spatially resolved perturbation models as an extension of existing single-cell perturbation models. Such models will enable in silico testing of microenvironment perturbations, including combinatorial perturbations. The ability to formulate causal hypotheses for the microenvironmental determinants of context-dependent cell states could revolutionize our understanding of the cell-cell interactions that underlie diverse phenomena, from stem cell niches to function and dysfunction of the immune system. Although deep learning models can often achieve unparalleled performance, it can be challenging to understand the factors that drive model predictions. These questions fall within the purview of model interpretability, an increasingly active area of research in the machine learning community. Interpretability is of paramount importance, not only for uncovering new biology but also for building confidence and trust, especially in clinical settings.Conclusion. Single-cell transcriptomics and spatial transcriptomics technologies are revolutionizing our understanding of the origins, regulation and maintenance of cell states within complex tissues. Through careful experimental design and strategic utilization of computational methods, researchers can harness the full potential of scRNA-seq and ST data to uncover crucial cell states and multicellular communities in myriad contexts. Despite the inherent challenges in data analysis, rapidly emerging experimental and computational tools, along with large collaborative efforts such as the Human Cell Atlas, Human Protein Atlas and the Human Tumor Atlas Network, are facilitating the contextualization of these cell states in the broader spectrum of human health and disease. As large volumes of genomic, spatial and temporal data are being integrated across single cells from diverse tissues, species and conditions, we are only beginning to unravel the mysteries of the cell.
